revision.text:
'''三昧耶形''' ('''さんまやぎょう'''/'''さまやぎょう''')とは、[[密教]]に於いて、[[仏]]を表す象徴物の事。'''三形''' ('''さんぎょう''')とも略称する。
ちなみに三昧耶とは[[サンスクリット]]で「約束」、「契約」などを意味するサマヤ(samaya)から転じた言葉で、どの仏をどの象徴物で表すかが経典によって予め「取り決められている」事に由来する。
伝統的には[[如来]]や[[菩薩]]などの仏の本誓、即ち衆生を救済するために起こした誓願を示したものと定義されている。
多くの場合、各仏の[[持物]]がそのままその仏を象徴する三昧耶形となる。 例えば[[不動明王]]なら利剣、[[聖観音]]なら蓮華、虚空蔵菩薩なら[[如意宝珠]]など。 また、通常持物を持たない如来の場合は特別の象徴物が三昧耶形とされる場合もあるが([[大日如来]]が[[宝塔]]など)、[[印相]]を以て三昧耶形とする場合もある。
[[曼荼羅]]などの仏画では、仏の絵姿の代わりに三昧耶形で描く事も多い。よく知られている例としては、[[金剛界曼荼羅]]の三昧耶会と降三世三昧耶会がある。

伝統的には[[如来]]や[[菩薩]]などの仏の本誓、即ち衆生を救済するために起こした誓願を示したものと定義されている。

{{Buddhism}}'''三昧耶形''' ('''さんまやぎょう'''/'''さまやぎょう''')とは、[[密教]]に於いて、[[仏]]を表す象徴物の事。'''三形''' ('''さんぎょう''')とも略称する。

Created page with "{{Cite notice|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}} '''Samaya Symbol''' (三昧耶形, ''Samayagyō'') refers to symbolic objects representing a [[Buddha]] in [[Esoteric Buddhism]]. It is also abbreviated as '''Sangyō''' (三形). Incidentally, ''samaya'' originates from the [[Sanskrit]] term "samaya," meaning "promise" or "contract," and it refers to the predetermined agreements in the scriptures about which symbolic object represents which Buddha. Traditionally,..."
revision.text:
{{Cite notice|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}} '''Samaya Symbol''' (三昧耶形, ''Samayagyō'') refers to symbolic objects representing a [[Buddha]] in [[Esoteric Buddhism]]. It is also abbreviated as '''Sangyō''' (三形).
Incidentally, ''samaya'' originates from the [[Sanskrit]] term "samaya," meaning "promise" or "contract," and it refers to the predetermined agreements in the scriptures about which symbolic object represents which Buddha.
Traditionally, it is defined as a representation of the primary vow of Buddhas such as [[Tathagata]]s or [[Bodhisattva]]s, signifying their pledge to save sentient beings.
In many cases, the symbolic objects (''[[Attributes|mochibutsu]]'') associated with each Buddha serve as their ''Samaya'' symbol. For example: - [[Fudō Myōō]] is represented by the [[Kurikara Sword]]. - [[Shō Kannon]] is represented by a lotus. - [[Kokūzō Bosatsu]] is represented by the [[wish-fulfilling jewel]].
For Tathagatas, who typically do not hold symbolic objects, special objects like the [[treasure tower]] for [[Dainichi Nyorai]] may be used as their ''Samaya'' symbols, or their [[Mudras|hand gestures]] (''insō'') may serve this purpose.
In Buddhist art such as [[mandalas]], Buddhas are often depicted through their ''Samaya'' symbols instead of their physical forms. Well-known examples include the ''Samaya'' Assembly and the ''Gozanze'' Samaya Assembly in the [[Vajradhatu Mandala]].
== Notes == {{Section stub|1=Notes in citation format|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}} {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == <!-- Please include only the references that were actually consulted during the editing of the main text --> {{Section stub|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}}

revision.comment: deciding to rename this category to a more descriptive one
revision.text:
{{Cite notice|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}} '''Samaya Symbol''' (三昧耶形, ''Samayagyō'') refers to symbolic objects representing a [[Buddha]] in [[Esoteric Buddhism]]. It is also abbreviated as '''Sangyō''' (三形).
Incidentally, ''samaya'' originates from the [[Sanskrit]] term "samaya," meaning "promise" or "contract," and it refers to the predetermined agreements in the scriptures about which symbolic object represents which Buddha.
Traditionally, it is defined as a representation of the primary vow of Buddhas such as [[Tathagata]]s or [[Bodhisattva]]s, signifying their pledge to save sentient beings.
In many cases, the symbolic objects (''[[Attributes|mochibutsu]]'') associated with each Buddha serve as their ''Samaya'' symbol. For example: - [[Fudō Myōō]] is represented by the [[Kurikara Sword]]. - [[Shō Kannon]] is represented by a lotus. - [[Kokūzō Bosatsu]] is represented by the [[wish-fulfilling jewel]].
For Tathagatas, who typically do not hold symbolic objects, special objects like the [[treasure tower]] for [[Dainichi Nyorai]] may be used as their ''Samaya'' symbols, or their [[Mudras|hand gestures]] (''insō'') may serve this purpose.
In Buddhist art such as [[mandalas]], Buddhas are often depicted through their ''Samaya'' symbols instead of their physical forms. Well-known examples include the ''Samaya'' Assembly and the ''Gozanze'' Samaya Assembly in the [[Vajradhatu Mandala]].
== Notes == {{Section stub|1=Notes in citation format|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}} {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:kk]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Cite notice|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}} '''Samaya Symbol''' (三昧耶形, ''Samayagyō'') refers to symbolic objects representing a [[Buddha]] in [[Esoteric Buddhism]]. It is also abbreviated as '''Sangyō''' (三形).
Incidentally, ''samaya'' originates from the [[Sanskrit]] term "samaya," meaning "promise" or "contract," and it refers to the predetermined agreements in the scriptures about which symbolic object represents which Buddha.
Traditionally, it is defined as a representation of the primary vow of Buddhas such as [[Tathagata]]s or [[Bodhisattva]]s, signifying their pledge to save sentient beings.
In many cases, the symbolic objects (''[[Attributes|mochibutsu]]'') associated with each Buddha serve as their ''Samaya'' symbol. For example: - [[Fudō Myōō]] is represented by the [[Kurikara Sword]]. - [[Shō Kannon]] is represented by a lotus. - [[Kokūzō Bosatsu]] is represented by the [[wish-fulfilling jewel]].
For Tathagatas, who typically do not hold symbolic objects, special objects like the [[treasure tower]] for [[Dainichi Nyorai]] may be used as their ''Samaya'' symbols, or their [[Mudras|hand gestures]] (''insō'') may serve this purpose.
In Buddhist art such as [[mandalas]], Buddhas are often depicted through their ''Samaya'' symbols instead of their physical forms. Well-known examples include the ''Samaya'' Assembly and the ''Gozanze'' Samaya Assembly in the [[Vajradhatu Mandala]].
== Notes == {{Section stub|1=Notes in citation format|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}} {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== References == <!-- Please include only the references that were actually consulted during the editing of the main text --> {{Section stub|date=2015年10月4日 (日) 11:56 (UTC)}}
== Related Topics == * [[Attributes]] * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|ja|仏教のシンボル}} * [[Mudras|Hand Gestures]] * [[Bīja (Esoteric Buddhism)|Bīja]]
{{Esoteric Buddhism2}} {{Buddhism2}}

{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in [[Esoteric Buddhism]] (密教) to represent specific [[Buddhas]] or [[Bodhisattvas]]. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the [[Sanskrit]] word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's [[attributes]] (持物). For example: * For [[Fudō Myōō]] (不動明王), the sword (倶利伽羅剣, Kurikara sword) serves as the samaya symbol. * For [[Shō Kannon]] (聖観音), a lotus blossom is used. * For [[Kokūzō Bosatsu]] (虚空蔵菩薩), the wish-fulfilling jewel (如意宝珠) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the [[Tathagata]]s ([[如来]]), special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * [[Dainichi Nyorai]] (大日如来) is often represented by a [[treasure stupa]] (宝塔). * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ([[Mudras|印相]]) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including [[mandalas]]. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The [[Vajradhatu Mandala]] (金剛界曼荼羅) within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' (降三世三昧耶会).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * [[Attributes in art|Attributes]] * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * [[Mudras]] * [[Siddham seed syllables|Seed syllables in Esoteric Buddhism]]
{{Esoteric Buddhism}} {{Buddhism}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]

Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.

For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Bump 1-20 (2)]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]

{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.

Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure stupa}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Samaya Symbols}} [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Esoteric Buddhism|ja|密教|12=draft}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=draft}} or {{ill|Bodhisattvas|ja|菩薩|12=draft}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|12=draft}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|12=draft}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王|12=draft}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|12=draft}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|12=draft}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩|12=draft}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠|12=draft}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来|12=draft}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来|12=draft}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure stupa|12=draft}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相|12=draft}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅|12=draft}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=draft}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=draft}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|12=draft}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相|12=draft}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}} <!--Samaya Symbols--> <!--Samaya Symbols-->

They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|qq=draft|qq=draft}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|1=Saint symbolism|2=ja|3=アトリビュート|lt=attributes|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. For example: * For {{ill|Fudō Myōō|ja|不動明王|1=Acala|lt=Fudō Myōō|2=ja|3=不動明王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Kokūzō Bosatsu|ja|虚空蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintāmaṇi|ja|如意宝珠|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathagata|ja|如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Dainichi Nyorai|ja|大日如来|1=Vairocana|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|2=ja|3=大日如来|4=zh|5=毗卢遮那佛|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudras|ja|印相|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|mandalas|ja|曼荼羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Mudras|ja|印相|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

revision.comment: Bot: append Wikidata-derived interwikis
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]] to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|qq=}} or [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]]. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|qq=}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|1=Saint symbolism|2=ja|3=アトリビュート|lt=attributes|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/attributes|13=User:Immanuelle/Saint symbolism|qq=}}. For example: * For [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurikara sword|qq=}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For [[:en:Ākāśagarbha|Kokūzō Bosatsu]], the wish-fulfilling jewel ([[:en:Cintamani|Cintāmaṇi]]) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the [[:en:Tathāgata|Tathagata]], special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]] is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ([[:en:Mudra|Mudras]]) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including [[:en:Mandala|mandalas]]. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|qq=}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|qq=}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|qq=}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * [[:en:Mudra|Mudras]] * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}
{{translated page|ja|三昧耶形|version=93769060|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist symbols]] [[Category:Esoteric Buddhism]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] }}

They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.

== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|qq=}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * [[:en:Mudra|Mudras]] * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

For instance: * [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]] is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ([[:en:Mudra|Mudras]]) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including [[:en:Mandala|mandalas]]. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|qq=|1=attribute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * [[:en:Mudra|Mudras]] * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:三昧耶形]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11355543]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]] to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}} or [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]]. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット|1=Sanskrit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11059}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|1=attribute|2=ja|3=アトリビュート|lt=attributes|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/attributes|13=User:Immanuelle/Saint symbolism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}}. For example: * For [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurikara sword|qq=}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For [[:en:Ākāśagarbha|Kokūzō Bosatsu]], the wish-fulfilling jewel ([[:en:Cintamani|Cintāmaṇi]]) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the [[:en:Tathāgata|Tathagata]], special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]] is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ([[:en:Mudra|Mudras]]) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including [[:en:Mandala|mandalas]]. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|1=attribute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * [[:en:Mudra|Mudras]] * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:三昧耶形]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11355543]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]] to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}} or [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]]. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット|1=Sanskrit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11059}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|1=attribute|2=ja|3=アトリビュート|lt=attributes|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/attributes|13=User:Immanuelle/Saint symbolism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}}. For example: * For [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurikara sword|qq=}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For [[:en:Ākāśagarbha|Kokūzō Bosatsu]], the wish-fulfilling jewel ([[:en:Cintamani|Cintāmaṇi]]) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the [[:en:Tathāgata|Tathagata]], special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]] is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ([[:en:Mudra|Mudras]]) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including [[:en:Mandala|mandalas]]. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|1=attribute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * [[:en:Mudra|Mudras]] * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:三昧耶形]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:三昧耶形]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11355543]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in [[:en:Mikkyō|Esoteric Buddhism]] to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}} or [[:en:Bodhisattva|Bodhisattvas]]. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット|1=Sanskrit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11059}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|1=attribute|2=ja|3=アトリビュート|lt=attributes|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/attributes|13=User:Immanuelle/Saint symbolism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}}. For example: * For [[:en:Acala|Fudō Myōō]], the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurikara sword|qq=}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For [[:en:Ākāśagarbha|Kokūzō Bosatsu]], the wish-fulfilling jewel ([[:en:Cintamani|Cintāmaṇi]]) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the [[:en:Tathāgata|Tathagata]], special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]] is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ([[:en:Mudra|Mudras]]) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including [[:en:Mandala|mandalas]]. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|1=attribute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * [[:en:Mudra|Mudras]] * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}
{{translated page|ja|三昧耶形|version=93769060|comment=Imported full ja history}}

For instance: * [[:en:Vairocana|Dainichi Nyorai]] is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ([[:en:Mudra|Mudras]]) may serve as samaya symbols.

Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|1=attribute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * [[:en:Mudra|Mudras]] * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.

For example: * For {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurikara sword|qq=}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|WD=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintamani|en|Cintamani|WD=Q2963837|lt=Cintāmaṇi}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathāgata|en|Tathāgata|WD=Q1061285|lt=Tathagata}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|WD=Q208030|lt=Mudras}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|Mandala|en|Mandala|WD=Q191431|lt=mandalas}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|1=attribute|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * {{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|WD=Q208030|lt=Mudras}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

{{Citation needed|date=October 4, 2015}} '''Samaya Symbols''' (三昧耶形, さんまやぎょう/さまやぎょう) are symbolic objects used in {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|WD=Q336102|lt=Esoteric Buddhism}} to represent specific {{ill|Buddhas|ja|仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhas|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏陀|1=Buddha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7055}} or {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|WD=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattvas}}. They are also referred to as '''Sanmyō''' (三形) in abbreviated form.
The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット|1=Sanskrit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11059}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|1=attribute|2=ja|3=アトリビュート|lt=attributes|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/attributes|13=User:Immanuelle/Saint symbolism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}}. For example: * For {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurikara sword|qq=}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|WD=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintamani|en|Cintamani|WD=Q2963837|lt=Cintāmaṇi}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathāgata|en|Tathāgata|WD=Q1061285|lt=Tathagata}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|WD=Q208030|lt=Mudras}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|Mandala|en|Mandala|WD=Q191431|lt=mandalas}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).

The term ''samaya'' originates from the {{ill|Sanskrit|ja|サンスクリット語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanskrit|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サンスクリット|1=Sanskrit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11059}} word for "promise" or "contract," indicating a prearranged agreement, as established in Buddhist scriptures, on which symbols represent specific Buddhas.
Traditionally, these symbols are defined as the manifestation of a Buddha’s vow or aspiration to save all sentient beings. Each symbol is inherently connected to the respective Buddha's spiritual essence.
== Characteristics == In most cases, the samaya symbol corresponds directly to the Buddha's or Bodhisattva's {{ill|attributes|ja|持物|1=Attribute (art)|2=ja|3=アトリビュート|lt=attributes|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/attributes|13=User:Immanuelle/Saint symbolism|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}}. For example: * For {{ill|Acala|en|Acala|WD=Q337624|lt=Fudō Myōō|1=Acala}}, the sword ({{ill|Kurikara sword|ja|倶利伽羅剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurikara sword|qq=}}) serves as the samaya symbol. * For {{ill|Shō Kannon|ja|聖観音|1=Āryāvalokiteśvara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shō Kannon|13=User:Immanuelle/Āryāvalokiteśvara|qq=}}, a lotus blossom is used. * For {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|WD=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}}, the wish-fulfilling jewel ({{ill|Cintamani|en|Cintamani|WD=Q2963837|lt=Cintāmaṇi|1=Cintamani}}) represents the samaya symbol.
In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathāgata|en|Tathāgata|WD=Q1061285|lt=Tathagata|1=Tathāgata}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|WD=Q208030|lt=Mudras|1=Mudra}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|Mandala|en|Mandala|WD=Q191431|lt=mandalas|1=Mandala}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
== References == {{Citation needed|section}} {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Attributes in art|ja|アトリビュート|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attributes in art|1=Attribute (art)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q758238}} * {{ill|Buddhist symbolism|en|Buddhist symbolism|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist symbolism|qq=}} * {{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|WD=Q208030|lt=Mudras|1=Mudra}} * [[:en:Seed_syllable|seed syllable]] {{Buddhism}}

In some instances, when a Buddha traditionally lacks attributes, such as in the case of the {{ill|Tathāgata|en|Tathāgata|WD=Q1061285|lt=Tathagata|1=Tathāgata}}, special objects are designated as their samaya symbols. For instance: * {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}} is often represented by a {{ill|treasure stupa|ja|宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|2=de|3=Schatzpagode|4=ja|5=宝塔|1=Treasure Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/treasure stupa|13=User:Immanuelle/Treasure Pagoda|qq=}}. * Alternatively, certain hand gestures ({{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|WD=Q208030|lt=Mudras|1=Mudra}}) may serve as samaya symbols.
== Usage in Mandalas == Samaya symbols are frequently depicted in Buddhist art, including {{ill|Mandala|en|Mandala|WD=Q191431|lt=mandalas|1=Mandala}}. Instead of depicting full-bodied Buddhas or Bodhisattvas, their respective symbols are used to represent them. Notable examples include: * The {{ill|Vajradhatu Mandala|ja|金剛界曼荼羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vajradhatu Mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 両界曼荼羅|1=Mandala of the Two Realms|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q468113}} within its Samaya Assembly (三昧耶会). * The ''Samaya Assembly of Trailokyavijaya'' ({{ill|降三世三昧耶会|ja|降三世三昧耶会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/降三世三昧耶会|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).
